description.value:
For the duration, you sense the presence of magic within 30 feet of you. If you sense magic in this way, you can use your action to see a faint aura around any visible creature or object in the area that bears magic, and you learn its school of magic, if any.

The spell can penetrate most barriers, but it is blocked by 1 foot of stone, 1 inch of common metal, a thin sheet of lead, or 3 feet of wood or dirt.

You cause a tremor in the ground within range. Each creature other than you in that area must make a [[/save dex format=long]]. On a failed save, a creature takes 1d6 bludgeoning damage and is knocked &Reference[prone]{prone}. If the ground in that area is loose earth or stone, it becomes difficult terrain until cleared, with each 5-foot-diameter portion requiring at least 1 minute to clear by hand.

At Higher Levels. When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 2nd level or higher, the damage increases by 1d6 for each slot level above 1st.

Grasping weeds and vines sprout from the ground in a 20-foot square starting from a point within range. For the duration, these plants turn the ground in the area into difficult terrain.

A creature in the area when you cast the spell must succeed on a [[/save str format=long]] or be &Reference[restrained]{restrained} by the entangling plants until the spell ends. A creature &Reference[restrained]{restrained} by the plants can use its action to make a [[/check str format=long]] against your spell save DC. On a success, it frees itself.

When the spell ends, the conjured plants wilt away.

description.value:
Each object in a 20-foot cube within range is outlined in blue, green, or violet light (your choice). Any creature in the area when the spell is cast is also outlined in light if it fails a [[/save dex format=long]]. For the duration, objects and affected creatures shed dim light in a 10-foot radius.

Any attack roll against an affected creature or object has advantage if the attacker can see it, and the affected creature or object can't benefit from being &Reference[invisible]{invisible}.

You evoke a fiery blade in your free hand. The blade is similar in size and shape to a scimitar, and it lasts for the duration. If you let go of the blade, it disappears, but you can evoke the blade again as a bonus action.

You can use your action to make a melee spell attack with the fiery blade. On a hit, the target takes 3d6 fire damage.

The flaming blade sheds bright light in a 10-foot radius and dim light for an additional 10 feet.

At Higher Levels. When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 4th level or higher, the damage increases by 1d6 for every two slot levels above 2nd.

materials.value: leaf of sumac

description.value:
A 5-foot-diameter sphere of fire appears in an unoccupied space of your choice within range and lasts for the duration. Any creature that ends its turn within 5 feet of the sphere must make a [[/save dex format=long]]. The creature takes 2d6 fire damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

As a bonus action, you can move the sphere up to 30 feet. If you ram the sphere into a creature, that creature must make the saving throw against the sphere's damage, and the sphere stops moving this turn.

When you move the sphere, you can direct it over barriers up to 5 feet tall and jump it across pits up to 10 feet wide. The sphere ignites flammable objects not being worn or carried, and it sheds bright light in a 20-foot radius and dim light for an additional 20 feet.

At Higher Levels. When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 3rd level or higher, the damage increases by 1d6 for each slot level above 2nd.

materials.value: a bit of tallow, a pinch of brimstone, and a dusting of powdered iron

description.value:
You create a 20-foot-radius sphere of fog centered on a point within range. The sphere spreads around corners, and its area is heavily obscured. It lasts for the duration or until a wind of moderate or greater speed (at least 10 miles per hour) disperses it.

At Higher Levels. When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 2nd level or higher, the radius of the fog increases by 20 feet for each slot level above 1st.

A &Reference[nat]{nature} spirit answers your call and transforms you into a powerful guardian. The transformation lasts until the spell ends. You choose one of the following forms to assume: Primal Beast or Great Tree.

Primal Beast. Bestial fur covers your body, your facial features become feral, and you gain the following benefits:

  • Your walking speed increases by 10 feet.
  • You gain &Reference[darkvision]{darkvision} with a range of 120 feet.
  • You make Strength-based attack rolls with advantage.
  • Your melee weapon attacks deal an extra 1d6 force damage on a hit.

Great Tree. Your skin appears barky, leaves sprout from your hair, and you gain the following benefits:

  • You gain 10 temporary hit points.
  • You make Constitution saving throws with advantage.
  • You make Dexterity- and Wisdom-based attack rolls with advantage.
  • While you are on the ground, the ground within 15 feet of you is difficult terrain for your enemies.

A line of strong wind 60 feet long and 10 feet wide blasts from you in a direction you choose for the spell's duration. Each creature that starts its turn in the line must succeed on a [[/save str format=long]] or be pushed 15 feet away from you in a direction following the line.

Any creature in the line must spend 2 feet of movement for every 1 foot it moves when moving closer to you.

The gust disperses gas or vapor, and it extinguishes candles, torches, and similar unprotected flames in the area. It causes protected flames, such as those of lanterns, to dance wildly and has a 50 percent chance to extinguish them.

As a bonus action on each of your turns before the spell ends, you can change the direction in which the line blasts from you.

materials.value: a legume seed

description.value:
You call forth a &Reference[nat]{nature} spirit to soothe the wounded. The intangible spirit appears in a space that is a 5-foot cube you can see within range. The spirit looks like a transparent beast or fey (your choice).

Until the spell ends, whenever you or a creature you can see moves into the spirit’s space for the first time on a turn or starts its turn there, you can cause the spirit to restore 1d6 hit points to that creature (no action required). The spirit can’t heal constructs or undead. The spirit can heal a number of times equal to 1 + your spellcasting ability modifier (minimum of twice). After healing that number of times, the spirit disappears.

As a bonus action on your turn, you can move the spirit up to 30 feet to a space you can see.

At Higher Levels. When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 3rd level or higher, the healing increases by 1d6 for each slot level above 2nd.

Choose a manufactured metal object, such as a metal weapon or a suit of heavy or medium metal armor, that you can see within range. You cause the object to glow red-hot. Any creature in physical contact with the object takes 2d8 fire damage when you cast the spell. Until the spell ends, you can use a bonus action on each of your subsequent turns to cause this damage again.

If a creature is holding or wearing the object and takes the damage from it, the creature must succeed on a [[/save con format=long]] or drop the object if it can. If it doesn’t drop the object, it has disadvantage on attack rolls and ability checks until the start of your next turn.

At Higher Levels. When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 3rd level or higher, the damage increases by 1d8 for each slot level above 2nd.

materials.value: a piece of iron and a flame

description.value:
Choose a humanoid that you can see within range. The target must succeed on a [[/save wis format=long]] or be &Reference[paralyzed]{paralyzed} for the duration. At the end of each of its turns, the target can make another [[/save wis format=long]]. On a success, the spell ends on the target.

At Higher Levels. When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 3rd level or higher, you can target one additional humanoid for each slot level above 2nd. The humanoids must be within 30 feet of each other when you target them.

description.value:
A hail of rock-hard ice pounds to the ground in a 20-foot-radius, 40-foot-high cylinder centered on a point within range. Each creature in the cylinder must make a [[/save dex format=long]]. A creature takes 2d8 bludgeoning damage and 4d6 cold damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

Hailstones turn the storm's area of effect into difficult terrain until the end of your next turn.

At Higher Levels. When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 5th level or higher, the bludgeoning damage increases by 1d8 for each slot level above 4th.

materials.value: a pinch of dust and a few drops of water

description.value:
Swarming, biting locusts fill a 20-foot-radius sphere centered on a point you choose within range. The sphere spreads around corners. The sphere remains for the duration, and its area is lightly obscured. The sphere's area is difficult terrain.

When the area appears, each creature in it must make a [[/save con format=long]]. A creature takes 4d10 piercing damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one. A creature must also make this saving throw when it enters the spell's area for the first time on a turn or ends its turn there.

At Higher Levels. When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 6th level or higher, the damage increases by 1d10 for each slot level above 5th.

description.value:
A wave of healing energy washes out from a point of your choice within range. Choose up to six creatures in a 30-foot-radius sphere centered on that point. Each target regains hit points equal to 3d8 + your spellcasting ability modifier. This spell has no effect on undead or constructs.

At Higher Levels. When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 6th level or higher, the healing increases by 1d8 for each slot level above 5th.

A silvery beam of pale light shines down in a 5-foot-radius, 40-foot-high cylinder centered on a point within range. Until the spell ends, dim light fills the cylinder.

When a creature enters the spell’s area for the first time on a turn or starts its turn there, it is engulfed in ghostly flames that cause searing pain, and it must make a [[/save con format=long]]. It takes 2d10 radiant damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

A shapechanger makes its saving throw with disadvantage. If it fails, it also instantly reverts to its original form and can’t assume a different form until it leaves the spell’s light.

On each of your turns after you cast this spell, you can use an action to move the beam up to 60 feet in any direction.

At Higher Levels. When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 3rd level or higher, the damage increases by 1d10 for each slot level above 2nd.

materials.value: several seeds of any moonseed plant and a piece of opalescent feldspar

description.value: A veil of shadows and silence radiates from you, masking you and your companions from detection. For the duration, each creature you choose within 30 feet of you (including you) has a +10 bonus to Dexterity (&Reference[ste]{Stealth}) checks and can't be tracked except by magical means. A creature that receives this bonus leaves behind no tracks or other traces of its passage.

materials.value: ashes from a burned leaf of mistletoe and a sprig of spruce

description.value:
Until the spell ends, freezing rain and sleet fall in a 20-foot-tall cylinder with a 40-foot radius centered on a point you choose within range. The area is heavily obscured, and exposed flames in the area are doused.

The ground in the area is covered with slick ice, making it difficult terrain. When a creature enters the spell's area for the first time on a turn or starts its turn there, it must make a [[/save dex format=long]]. On a failed save, it falls &Reference[prone]{prone}.

If a creature starts its turn in the spell's area and is concentrating on a spell, the creature must make a successful [[/save con format=long]] against your spell save DC or lose concentration.

The ground in a 20-foot radius centered on a point within range twists and sprouts hard spikes and thorns. The area becomes difficult terrain for the duration. When a creature moves into or within the area, it takes 2d4 piercing damage for every 5 feet it travels.

The transformation of the ground is camouflaged to look natural. Any creature that can't see the area at the time the spell is cast must make a Wisdom (&Reference[prc]{Perception}) check against your spell save DC to recognize the terrain as hazardous before entering it.

description.value:
You call forth a bestial spirit. It manifests in an unoccupied space that you can see within range. This corporeal form uses the Bestial Spirit stat block. When you cast the spell, choose an environment: Air, Land, or Water. The creature resembles an animal of your choice that is native to the chosen environment, which determines certain traits in its stat block. The creature disappears when it drops to 0 hit points or when the spell ends.

The creature is an ally to you and your companions. In combat, the creature shares your initiative count, but it takes its turn immediately after yours. It obeys your verbal commands (no action required by you). If you don’t issue any, it takes the Dodge action and uses its move to avoid danger.

At Higher Levels. When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 3rd level or higher, use the higher level wherever the spell’s level appears in the stat block.

In the age of legend, Lugdana, an orphan, found shelter in the embrace of Lord Argynvost, who nurtured her as his own. He, a master of war, tactics, and blade, imparted his wisdom. She embraced these teachings, becoming a formidable warrior.

When Lord Argynvost established the Order of the Silver Dragon to guard Barovia, Lugdana sought to join. Though her skill was unquestioned, the lord hesitated out of love, fearing her unwavering determination might lead to her demise. She reminded him of his own words, "How you live is as important as how you die!" She passed the Knight's Challenge, becoming Dame Lugdana Argynvost, the only knight bearing that name.

Her valor earned renown, as she never shrank from battle, always leading the charge. In times of peace, she tended to her fellow knights, the elderly, and infirm at the Abbey in Krezk.

Lugdana found favor at the Abbey, growing close to Sister Constance and Blessed Sister Markovia. Even the esteemed Countess von Hapsburg respected her and listened to her counsel. When asked why she didn't rest at Argynvostholt, Lugdana explained her unwavering commitment to serving Barovia, tending to the sick, and defending against enemies. Her sole pleasure lay in service.

But the tides turned. Count von Hapsburg and his knights fell in a dishonorable ambush at the Battle of Berez. Strahd declared Argynvost and his knights traitors, and tension escalated. Argynvost prepared for a siege at Argynvostholt.

Now, the Silver Dragon knights fought two adversaries: the Terg forces which the valley had battled for a quarter of a century, and Strahd's forces who lay claim to the throne, both seeking Argynvost and his knight’s annihilation and erasure from history. Argynvost divided his forces between Tsolenka Pass and Argynvostholt, prioritizing the defense of Tsolenka Pass and the protection of the Amber Temple.

At the Battle of Raven River, Lugdana faced a grievous wound and was taken to the Abbey for care. Sister Constance suggested it was time for her to rest, she had proven herself as a valiant knight, but Lugdana couldn't comply. She uncovered a new threat in Barovia, the undead who began terrorizing the valley. Her concern for the Barovian peasants drove her to recover swiftly. As she prepared to return to battle, she prayed not for herself or victory but for her service to bring peace to Barovia, echoing her father's wisdom: "How we live is as important as how we die."

Hearing Lugdana's prayer, Sister Constance bestowed upon her the Holy Symbol of the Sun, an ancient relic with a forgotten history, secretly treasured by the Abbey. With this symbol gracing her, Lugdana fearlessly confronted hordes of undead, emerging as the celebrated Knight of Light, a hero to the beleaguered people in the war-ravaged realm of Barovia.

Lugdana had emerged as a formidable adversary against Strahd’s undead forces. Strahd seized one hundred villagers, demanding her surrender or a villager would be executed daily. As her fellow knights set out to free the captives, Lugdana remained at Argynvostholt with her father, Lord Argynvost.

Strahd cunningly anticipated the knights' actions, which left Argynvostholt unguarded. In the cover of night, Strahd infiltrated the keep, slaying Lugdana as she slept and defeating Lord Argynvost. The Holy Symbol of the Sun eluded his grasp, disappearing and returning miraculously to Sister Constance at the Abbey. Lugdana's spirit cast a curse upon Strahd, condemning him to an unending existence devoid of genuine love and solace.

In his rage, Strahd desecrated the body of Lord Argynvost, the Silver Dragon, taking his skull as a morbid trophy to Castle Ravenloft, denying the knights a proper burial for their lord.

A haunting whisper, bearing the words "Strahd Must Die," lingers in the Abbey and Argynvostholt—a testament to Lugdana's indomitable spirit. Some believed she had become a Revenant, hunting the undead as she did in life.

Long ago, Lord Argynvost, a Silver Dragon in noble guise, graced the valley. Wealthy beyond measure and friend to the Fey, he honored their guardianship of the land with frequent visits and gifts. A premonition of war and a discovery of the Amber Temple's Dark Powers set Argynvost on edge; if released or seized by evil, these powers could doom the valley. Knowing the temple's wizards alone couldn't withstand a siege, he took action.

Argynvost had sought the acclaimed Ursari masons to construct his stronghold, Argynvostholt, and the Tsolenka Pass Gates to protect against those seeking the temple's dark secrets. To bolster defenses, Argynvost endowed the Order of the Dragon with his riches, enlisting a band of paladin knights. Together, they formed the Order of the Silver Dragon, sworn to shield the valley and thwart the dire fate that would follow should the Amber Temple be breached by the enemy.

In Barovia's lush lands, the Knights of Argynvost were a vigilant force, sworn to the Order of the Dragon. Their sacred mission was dual: to defend their home and covertly guard the Amber Temple. Together with the Shield Maidens, they kept watch over Tsolenka Pass, their allegiance to Argynvost unyielding, their ultimate duty to shield the temple.

War's shadow loomed as a young prince, hungry for power, challenged Barovia's peace. Yet, against the resolute Order of the Silver Dragon, he faltered. Lacking the guile and might of Argynvost's paladins, his campaign failed.

In his obsession, the prince exposed Argynvost's secret draconian identity. With a treacherous plan, he lured the knights away from their stronghold at Argynvostholt. Lord Argynvost, with only his brave daughter, Lady Lugdana, remained at their stronghold. The prince secretly entered Argynvostholt, where he executed Lady Lugdana in her sleep and slew Argynvost. The dragon's skull, a symbol of the prince’s victory, was taken to Castle Ravenloft.

Returning to their stronghold, the Knights found tragedy instead of solace. In their weariness and bereft of magic granted to them by their Lord Argynvost, they were ambushed and slaughtered by the prince's army. The fallen knights’ heads were displayed at the gates of Barovia, a testament to the prince's ruthless ascent.

Amidst the bloodshed, Sir Godfrey emerged as the sole survivor, a bearer of grief and grave tidings, escaping to the Abbey of St. Martikov. In that hallowed refuge, he recounted the valiant but doomed stand of Argynvost and his knights, a tale now etched into Barovia's somber annals.

Barovia grieved under the dark prince's cruel shadow, the destiny of the Amber Temple—and all it held—teetering at the edge of ruin. Argynvost's legacy, once a shining symbol of hope, seemed to wane in the growing gloom, a stark reminder of the cost of conflict and the vulnerability of honor.

In Castle Ravenloft's grim depths, Vladimir Horngaard, the lone captive knight, endured the dark prince's vile tortures. Before Vladimir’s scheduled death, the prince gloated over Argynvost's fate, taunting Vladimir with the grotesque trophy of the dragon's skull. In a last act of defiance, Vladimir cursed the prince, swearing the knights' vengeance would be inexorable.

Defying mortality, Horngaard's spirit arose as a revenant, consumed with a relentless thirst for revenge. This passion summoned his brethren in arms, resurrecting them as revenants to join his undying crusade. Their collective resolve, unbreakable even in death, became a spectral legion hellbent on avenging their fallen lord and reclaiming their lost honor.

Sir Godfrey, upon learning the prince had tapped into the Dark Powers at the Amber Temple and the capture of a Fey, embarked on a doomed rescue. His attempt, though brave, ended as the dark prince ruthlessly ended his mortal coil. Godfrey joined Horngaard's legion in spectral defiance.

The revenant knights of Horngaard, ethereal and relentless, struck terror into the prince's ranks. In Barovia's grip, they fell only to rise anew, their spirits inhabiting fresh corpses to wage their tireless war. Outnumbered but undaunted, their campaign spanned decades, their enemies felled in droves, yet Strahd remained consumed by darker pursuits, leaving his minions to Horngaard's wrath.

As darkness grew, the revenants faced overwhelming evil, their plight seeming dire. Yet, neither defeat nor the mists that bound them could quench their vengeful fire. With no peace to find, Vladimir steered his ghostly knights in a vengeful crusade against those aligned with the prince's tyranny. Over centuries, their quest for vengeance darkened their once-honorable intent, and now, whispers tell of Vladimir's restless presence in Argynvostholt's ruins, and his knights' quest for redemption eclipsed by an unyielding hatred.

Argynvost's spirit, the noble dragon, remains unrested, aware of his knights' fall from grace. He reaches through dreams to the worthy, beseeching them to guide his knights towards tranquility.

The visions convey a path to redemption: Vladimir's peace hinges on returning the dragon's skull to Argynvostholt's mausoleum. Should this be done, Argynvost's essence will rise to the manor's highest spire, transforming into a beacon of celestial light that bathes Barovia in its glow. This divine luminescence touches Vladimir's soul, reminding him of forsaken virtues. Overwhelmed, Vladimir kneels, weeping, pleading with higher powers to free his knights from their accursed existence. This act of remorse grants the knights a semblance of peace, their vengeance quelled, though their spirits remain bound to Barovia.

Argynvostholt, once a symbol of honor, now lies in ruin, a haunted shell echoing past glories. Its reputation for peril keeps the living at bay. The spirits within, though not malevolent, are weary of life's intrusions and fiercely guard their solitude. Over time, few dared to disturb the silence of this mournful place.

My knights have fallen, and this land is lost. The armies of my enemy will not be stopped by sword or spell, claw or fang. Today I will die, not for avenging those who have fallen, but defending that which I love- this valley, this home, and the ideals of the Order of the Silver Dragon.

The evil surrounds me. The time has come to throw off this guise and show these heathens my true fearsome form. Let it spark terror in their hearts! Let them tell their stories of dark triumph against the protector of the Balinok Mountains! Let Argynvost be remembered as a dragon of honor and valor. My one regret is that my remains will not lie in their rightful place, in the hallowed mausoleum of Argynvostholt. No doubt my bones will be scattered among my enemies like the coins of a plundered hoard, trophies of a hard-won victory.

I do not fear death. Though my body will die, my spirit will live on. Let it serve as a beacon of light against the darkness. Let it bring hope to a land wrought with despair.

Now, to battle!

Battle of Berez

Fall of the Falkon

 

Penned by

Burgomaster of Berez

Lazlo Ulrich

I record these words so that future generations will remember the sacrifice of those who gave their lives to protect our beloved valley and the truth of the turning point of the war.

 

The year 346 was filled with tragedy and has become known to Barovians as the Dark Year.

 

The Great War had plagued Barovia for 26 years. My son was born when the war started, grew up during the war, and eventually served as a Knight of the Order of the Silver Dragon. My son has only known war and never the beautiful time of peace.

 

The Terg forces had set camp at Tser Lake, west of the falls. They had been planning to march across the Berez fields, cross the Luna River, conquer Berez and destroy Argynvostholt, the Knights of Order of the Silver Dragon’s seat of power. It was believed if Berez and Argynvostholt fell, the war would be over and the Terg would claim Barovia.

 

Lord Falkon von Hapsburg called forth a battle council at Argynvostholt. Lord Argynvost was away at Tsolenka Pass and his daughter, Lady Lugdana would take his seat at the council. King Barov of Cadia was also away, and his son Prince Strahd von Zarovich would assume his father's seat. 

 Battle Plans

Lord Hapsburg’s plan was for the Hapsburg army to cross east of the Luna River and draw out the Terg forces into the open fields of Berez. Prince Strahd would lead the Cadia army from the north near Vallaki, cutting off the Terg Forces from a retreat. Lord Argynvost’s knights would arrive from Tsolenka Pass, taking the precarious route down to the Luna River, and attack from the South. Surrounding the Terg forces and destroying their army.

The plan was risky, to draw out the Terg forces it needed to be worthwhile and that was Lord Falkon von Hapsburg’s role. The Lord of Barovia would lead a small force across the fields of Berez, a prize the Terg would certainly seize upon, drawing them out and exposing their north and south flanks. If the battle was successful; it would mean the end of the war.

I bid farewell to my son and Lady Lugdana, who accompanied Lord Falkon von Hapsburg. The Hapsburg army made camp on the east side of the Luna River. Lord Hapsburg made sure information was passed to the Terg that Lord Hapsburg was planning a surprise attack.

 The Battle of Berez

The fall of the Falkon

On the cool spring morning of the battle, I stood on the shores of the Luna and watched as my son and the Lord of Barovia rode off across the fields of Berez to meet the Terg.

Lord Hapsburg’s ruse worked; the bulk of the Terg forces marched out of their camp to meet Lord Hapsburg on the battlefield. Prince Strahd to the north overlooking the battlefield, waited for the signal. While Lord Argynvost’s forces were marching up from the south.

When the signal was given, Lord Hapsburg led the charge towards the massive Terg forces, outnumbering Lord Hapsburg’s army by hundred to one. Yet Prince Strahd remained motionless and did not join in the battle. Prince Strahd sat upon his horse, watching the battle unfold.

Lord’s Hapsburg’s forces were surrounded. A Terg dismounted Lord Hapsburg and soon he found himself alone, surrounded by hundreds of Terg. Hapsburg’s forces pressed hard to get to their Lord, but to no avail.

The Terg fired six arrows into Lord Hapsburg before engaging him in hand-to-hand combat, and when they did, he struck down four of the Terg. Lord Hapsburg would not surrender and would not fall. A final arrow flew, piercing Lord Hapsburg’s eye and striking him dead. The Terg forces chopped off his head and removed his crown, leaving his mangled body on the battlefield before they returned to their camp.

Betrayal

Prince Strahd watched the events play out and when Lord Hapsburg fell, he slowly turned and rode away.

Lord Argynvost arrived as the sun was setting, but it was too late. Lord Argynvost had been delayed as a massive force laid siege on the Gates of Tsolenka.

Lord Argynvost, Lady Lugdana, and my son ventured out to the battlefield to retrieve Lord Falkon von Hapsburg’s remains.  He was given a stately funeral and entombed in Castle Barovia.

Prince Strahd von Zarovich declared his forces were attacked by the Terg and thus was unable to advance. He blamed Lord Argynvost for not coming to Lord Hapsburg's aid.

No one knew where the Terg managed to garner such a large army to attack the Tsolenka Gate at the same time they fought Lord Hapsburg on the fields of Berez.

Lord Argynvost declared it was Prince Strahd von Zarovich’s army that attacked the Gates of Tsolenka and was made to have us think it was the Terg. With Lord Falkon von Hapsburg, Lord of Barovia, dead and with no male heirs, King Barov of Cadia and his son Prince Strahd would declare rightful rule over Barovia, and so they did.

Lord Argynvost commanded the Hapsburg forces for the remainder of the war but became an enemy of House Zarovich.

biography.value:
Backstory

My earliest memory is being alone in the forest. I had wandered for days with little food and water. Alone and near death I found myself in the darkness surrounded by glowing eyes and snarling teeth. I knew that I was doomed and I braced myself for death. Just as my life was certainly coming to an end, I heard the sound of arrows whipping through the trees. My next memory is is waking up on a on a soft bed of leaves, elves whispering softly in the background. I finally felt safe and warm. I was always grateful to the tribe for saving my life and taking me in. The wood elves of the Size tribe lived in the wilderness, far from the cities and towns of the human races. They lived a simple life of seasonal foraging and migration. They were the protectors of the forest and the creatures within. I always had food, water, and a place to rest my bones, but I never truly felt that I belonged. Despite those feelings I was happy and lived in peace and harmony with the land and my tribe. I learned the druidic arts from the elders and continued their traditions. After a long trip to the edge of the wilds I returned to our camp very late after getting trapped for some time by the morning lord's wrath. To my horror the camp had been burned to the ground and everyone had had been slaughtered. For several years after I wandered the wilderness alone living off of the land. One day I stumbled upon a band of adventurers that had nearly died from starvation. I nursed them back to health and when they recovered I decided to stay with them as they traveled. Time went on and I found myself traveling further and further away from home. I visited many distant lands as the years passed. I learned many new skills and the languages of the locals. Most recently a vision has put me back on the path to my homeland. I knew that this was a journey that I must make without my friends. I slipped away from camp late one night making sure that I could not be followed. I don't know exactly where this path leads but I know where the path starts... a small village in Barovia.
